conf-meta.conf-name: 2025 10th International Conference on Energy Efficiency and Agricultural Engineering (EE&AE)

title-group.article-title: Understanding the Drivers of Organic Food Consumption: a Machine Learning Approach Towards Consumer Behaviour

conf-article-meta.aff:
Rajendra Mishra School of Engineering \\ Entrepreneurship \\ Indian Institute of Technology \\ Kharagpur \\ Kharagpur
, India \\ surajiitkgp18@gmail.com
Agricultural and Food Engineering \\ Department \\ Indian Institute of Technology \\ Kharagpur \\ Kharagpur, India \\ debasruti63@gmail.com
Department of Electrical & Electronics Engineering \\ Birla Institute of Technology and Science, Pilani - Hyderabad Campus \\ Hyderabad, India \\ areonbanerjee361@gmail.com

permissions.copyright-statement: 979-8-3315-1299-6 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

abstract.p: This study uses advanced machine learning techniques to investigate consumer behaviour towards organic food consumption in India. Data were collected through an online survey distributed across various social media platforms, resulting in $\mathbf{1, 9 0 7}$ respondents from different Indian states. The research focused on key factors, including knowledge, motivation, subjective norms, health, price, availability, consumption behaviour, attitude, and behaviour control of participants. A comparative analysis was conducted using multiple machine learning algorithms: logistic regression, support vector machines (SVM), k-nearest neighbors, random forest, naive Bayes, neural networks, and decision trees. After using five evaluation metrics, the decision tree model demonstrated superior performance, achieving a mean rank of 1.8. Additionally, these models ranked the variables affecting behavioural intention. Price and Motivation emerged as the most significant predictors of organic food consumption behavior. The findings contribute to understanding organic food consumption patterns and can inform marketing strategies and policy decisions in the organic food industry. Future research aims to refine and deploy these models for practical implementation, potentially enhancing decision-making processes in the organic food market.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The increasing global awareness of health, environmental sustainability, and food safety has led to a growing interest in organic food consumption [1]. In India, a country with deeprooted agricultural traditions and a rapidly evolving consumer base, the organic food market has experienced significant growth in recent years [2]. Despite this positive trend, organic food adoption rates remain uneven across different regions and demographic segments [3]. Understanding the behavioral factors that influence consumer intent to purchase and consume organic food is crucial for promoting sustainable consumption and informing policy, marketing, and production strategies [4].

Consumer behaviour towards organic food is a complex interplay of psychological, social, economic, and demographic factors [5]. Previous studies have identified various constructs, such as health consciousness, price sensitivity, knowledge, availability, motivation, and social influence that shape individual decisions in this domain [6]. However, traditional analytical methods often fail to capture such multidimensional data's nuanced patterns and non-linear relationships. Advanced machine learning (ML) techniques offer a promising alternative by enabling more accurate prediction, deeper insight, and the ability to model interactions between numerous variables [7].

This study uses a data-driven approach to explore the underlying factors influencing organic food consumption in India. By leveraging a robust dataset collected through a nationwide online survey, we apply a suite of ML algorithms, including Decision Trees, Random Forest, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Naive Bayes, and Neural Networks, to identify and evaluate the predictors of consumer intention. The study also ranks the relative importance of behavioral constructs such as knowledge, motivation, subjective norms, price, health, and availability, providing empirical insights that can inform stakeholders across the organic food supply chain.

Through rigorous data pre-processing, feature engineering, and model evaluation, this research seeks to answer two key questions: (1) Which factors most significantly influence the intention to consume organic food in the future? (2) Which ML model most accurately predicts this behavioral intention? The findings of this study will not only contribute to the academic discourse on sustainable consumption and serve as a practical guide for marketers, policymakers, and organic food producers aiming to align their strategies with consumer needs and expectations in India's diverse and dynamic market.

sec.label: A.
sec.title: Data Description and Collection
sec.p: This study investigates consumer behaviour towards organic food consumption in India using advanced ML techniques. Data were meticulously collected through online surveys via Google Forms distributed across various Indian states. The dataset consists of 1,907 respondents, and each survey form consists of 31 statements that capture a wide array of variables relevant to understanding consumer behaviour towards organic food. The questionnaire was designed to gather comprehensive information about the participants, focusing on key factors such as knowledge, motivation, subjective norms, health, price, availability, consumption behaviour, attitude, and behaviour control which were used as different independent variables (Table I) [8]. A five-point Likert scale was used for all the behavioral variables (1: strongly disagree, 5: strongly agree). In this study, the Behavioral Intention towards the consumption of organic food products was considered as the dependent variable.

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Data Pre- Processing
sec.p:
After the initial data collection, the data were preprocessed by removing the null-valued rows and outlier removal using the Interquartile Range (IQR) Technique in XLSTAT. Further, the pre-processed data was cleaned to generate the final dataset, which was further divided into training and testing sets at a ratio of $80: 20$. For classification purposes, the output variable was binarized using a threshold criterion: values greater than 2.5 were coded as 1 (indicating intention to consume organic products), while values less than or equal to 2.5 were coded as 0 (indicating no intention to consume organic products).
Table I. Constructs, items. and statements
Construct Item Code Statement
Availability A1 Organic food products are sufficiently available.
A2 There is a lack of clear labelling indicating organic products.
A3 The availability of organic food products on the market is limited.
Attitude AT1 I think consuming organic food is a healthier choice.
AT2 I believe organic food products have superior taste and quality.
AT3 I think organic food products are an environmentally friendly option.
AT4 I think people will buy more organic products if more incentives or subsidies are provided.
Behavioral Control BC1 I believe that converting to complete organic food consumption is possible.
BC2 It is fully my decision to consume organic food products.
BC3 I am capable enough to buy organic food.
Behavioral Intentions BI1 I intend to buy organic food products in future.
BI2 I will recommend others to buy organic food products.
BI3 I am always interested in buying more organic food for the family's needs.
BI4 I am committed to consuming organic products, in spite of the increased cost.
Consumption Behavior CB1 I buy organic products frequently.
Table I. Constructs, items. and statements
CB2 I will consume more organic food products for my better health in future.
CB3 I am trying to increase the number of organic food products in my daily life.
Health H1 Conventionally grown food may have harmful effects on human health.
H2 I will buy organic products for better health and quality.
H3 After Covid-19, I have become more serious about healthier food options.
Knowledge K1 I have sufficient knowledge about organic food production.
K2 It is important to consume organic food and it is safer to eat.
Motivation M1 The positive health effect of organic food products motivated me towards buying it.
M2 Less harmful effects on the environment motivated me to buy organic products.
M3 Better taste and superior quality of organic food motivated me to buy it.
Price P1 Organic food products are expensive.
P2 The price of organic food is extremely important in my decision to buy them.
Subjective Norms SN1 My friends, family and neighbors encouraged me to consume organic food.
SN2 Social media and online platforms influenced me to start buying organic food products.
SN3 I came to know more about organic food production from worldwide research.
SN4 Government initiatives on organic farming have influenced me.
C. Model Framework
Fig. 1.
Model framework
Seven ML models, namely Random Forest, SVM, Logistic Regression, KNN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes and Neural Network, are implemented in the study, and different performance metrics were implemented to validate the models (Fig. 1). The models were selected to conduct a comprehensive comparison across a diverse range of ML techniques. This was achieved by choosing a representative algorithm from each major classification family, such as treebased, linear, probabilistic, and neural network methods.
Random Forest: It is an ensemble learning method that constructs multiple decision trees during training and outputs the mode of the classes for classification. It improves accuracy and controls overfitting by averaging multiple trees [9].
Support Vector Machine (SVM): SVM is a supervised learning model that finds the hyperplane that best separates the feature space classes. It is effective in high-dimensional spaces and uses kernel functions to handle non-linear relationships [10].
Logistic Regression: It is a linear model used for binary classification. It estimates the probability that a given input belongs to a particular class by applying a logistic function to a linear combination of input features [11].
K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): KNN is a simple, instancebased learning algorithm that classifies a data point based on the majority class among its k-nearest neighbours in the feature space. It is intuitive and effective for small datasets [12].
Decision Tree: A Decision Tree is a tree-structured model where each node represents a feature, each branch represents a decision rule, and each leaf represents an outcome. It is easy to interpret and can handle numerical and categorical data [9].
Naive Bayes: It is a probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem. It assumes independence between features and is particularly effective for high-dimensional data. It is simple, fast, and works well for text classification tasks [13].
Neural Network: A Neural Network consists of interconnected layers of neurons, with each connection having a weight that adjusts during training. It can model complex non-linear relationships and is particularly powerful for image and speech recognition tasks [14].
Further, the performance of the ML models was measured by employing five metrics. The performance metrics were selected on the basis of their relevance and suitability for binary classification problems. The performance metrics used in this study are Accuracy, F1 Score, Precision, Recall, Area under the receiver operating curve (AUC), and finally, the mean rank of the model was reported.

sec.label: A.
sec.title: Comparisons of Ml Techniques
sec.p: The out-of-sample performance of seven supervised classifiers on our binary organic food consumption behavioural intention was reported in Table II. We evaluate each model using five standard metrics: Accuracy, F1-Score, Precision, Recall (Sensitivity) and AUC-ROC. The Decision Tree achieves the highest overall discriminative power (AUC$\text{ROC}=0.556$). It leads in Accuracy (57.3%) and balanced F1-

Score (0.643), indicating its ability to classify both positive and negative intent at various thresholds correctly. The Random Forest yields the strongest F1-Score (0.713) and exceptional Recall (92.6%), demonstrating that it captures nearly all true positive cases, albeit at the expense of lower Precision (58.0%) and a sub-par AUC-ROC (0.496), suggesting poor probability calibration. The Neural Network offers a close second in F1-Score (0.648) with balanced Precision and Recall (59.9 % and 70.5 %, respectively), while Logistic Regression and SVM deliver moderate but consistent performance across all metrics. KNN and Naive Bayes perform least effectively, with Accuracy near random-guess levels (50.0 % and 49.3%) and lower F1-scores (0.521 and 0.538).

The ROC-curve plot (Fig. 2) overlays the true-positive vs. false-positive trade-off for seven classifiers against the 45° "chance" line ($\text{AUC}=0.50$). SVM achieves the highest area under the curve (AUC $=0.55$), indicating it best distinguishes between positive and negative purchase intentions among the tested models. KNN (AUC $=0.53$) and Random Forest (AUC $=0.52)$ follow closely, showing only modest gains over random guessing. Decision Tree ($\text{AUC}=0.50$) performs at the chance level, while Logistic Regression ($\text{AUC}=0.46$), Naive Bayes (AUC $=0.48$), and Neural Network (AUC $=0.43$) fall below or barely above the baseline, reflecting poor discrimination. These results imply that if the objective is maximal detection of potential organic-food consumers, Random Forest is preferable. The Decision Tree is the superior choice if overall classification quality and thresholdagnostic ranking matter most.
Table II. Comparison of ml models
Model Accura cy F1Score Precisi on Recall AUCROC Mea n Ran k
Decisio n Tree 0.573 0.643 0.627 0.659 0.556 1.8
Rando m Forest 0.566 0.713 0.580 0.926 0.496 2.8
Neural Networ k 0.553 0.648 0.599 0.705 0.510 2.6
Logisti c Regress ion 0.540 0.583 0.618 0.551 0.515 3.6
SVM 0.520 0.589 0.588 0.591 0.489 5.5
KNearest Neighb ors 0.500 0.521 0.590 0.466 0.490 6.0
Naive Bayes 0.493 0.538 0.574 0.506 0.496 6.0
Fig. 2.
Roc curves for different ml models

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Comparisons of the Constructs in Different Models
sec.p:
The Built-in feature importance in scikit-learn implementation (Python) was used to identify and rank the independent variables in each model. Table III reports each model's ranked importance of the nine variables. Across treebased and linear classifiers in Random Forest, SVM, and Logistic Regression, Price consistently emerges as the top predictor (rank 1), followed by Motivation in Random Forest and Consumption Behaviour in both SVM and Logistic Regression. This indicates that the willingness to pay more for organic products plays a crucial role in purchase or consumption intention in these models [15]. Previous literature has also identified price as a major challenge that stops people from buying organic food products [16]. Therefore, reducing the price can further attract more consumers towards organic food and encourage farmers to move forward with green production. Motivation follows closely with the top four ranks in Random Forest (2), Logistic Regression (4), KNN (2), Decision tree (1), Naive Bayes (4) and Neural network (4), highlighting the role of motivation towards health and environmental concern of an individual in intention to buy it [17]. It corroborates a study by [18], where motivation played one of the most crucial roles in behavioral intention to purchase organic food products according to the Theory of Planned Behaviour and Protection Motivation Theory. Subjective Norms also rank among the top five for SVM (3), Logistic regression (5), KNN (5), Decision tree (5) and Naive Bayes (3), underscoring the role of social influence in purchase intent [19] [20]. However, a study by [21] found subjective norms to have barely a significant effect on the purchase intention of consumers. In contrast, Knowledge uniformly occupies the lowest ranks across almost all models, indicating that it contributes little to predictive performance [22]. It suggested that knowledge increases trust and improves attitudes towards organic food products, but several challenges, such as price, can limit the intention. However, the Neural Network uniquely elevates Availability to the highest rank (1), suggesting its sensitivity to market-access variables. In contrast, the KNN classifier highlights Behaviour Control (1) above all others, even though they showed lower ranks in all other models. These divergences reveal that different algorithms emphasize distinct secondary factors such as social norms, accessibility, or self-efficacy when modelling the consumer intention.
Table III. Ranks of constructs in different ml models
Variable Rand om Fores t SV M Logisti c Regres sion K N N Decis ion Tree Nai ve Ba yes Neur al Netw ork
Knowle dge 4 9 9 9 3 2 8
Motivati on 2 6 4 2 1 4 4
Subjecti ve Norms 9 3 5 5 5 3 6
Health 8 4 6 6 2 6 2
Price 1 1 1 4 9 5 7
Availabi lity 3 5 7 8 6 8 1
Consum ption Behavio ur 7 2 2 3 7 7 9
Attitude 6 7 3 7 4 9 3
Behavio ur Control 5 8 8 1 8 1 5

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study utilized advanced ML techniques to analyze consumer behaviour towards organic food consumption in India, leveraging data collected from 1,907 participants across various states, which, upon cleaning, came up to 1704. Key factors such as Knowledge, Motivation, Subjective norms, Health, Price, Availability, Consumption Behaviour, Attitudes, and Behaviour Control were examined to understand their influence on organic food consumption.

The comparative analysis of ML models demonstrated that decision trees provided the highest accuracy in predicting future consumption intent, highlighting the potential of ML techniques in uncovering complex patterns in consumer behaviour. These insights offer practical implications for policymakers, marketers, and stakeholders in the organic food sector, enabling them to design targeted interventions, awareness campaigns, and distribution strategies that resonate with consumer values and concerns.

This research offers a predictive tool for industry and makes informed policy decisions. Understanding the nuanced factors affecting organic food consumption can guide efforts to promote sustainable food choices and address consumer concerns effectively. Future research directions include refining these predictive models for practical implementation and exploring additional variables or methodologies to enhance decision-making processes in the organic food market.
